one_line_takeaway: A clinically-grounded 3-class risk taxonomy plus lightweight (4B/8B) fine-tuned guardrail classifiers, trained on synthetic clinician-patient dialogues and evaluated on a psychologist-annotated benchmark, outperform larger general-purpose safety classifiers (Llama Guard 3, gpt-oss-safeguard) on both turn-level AUROC/FPR and system-level automated-red-teaming attack-success/harmful-engagement metrics for multi-turn mental-health chatbot safety.
key_finding: On MindGuard-testset, MindGuard 8B reaches 0.982 AUROC (MindGuard 4B: 0.981), versus Llama Guard 3 8B 0.970, gpt-oss-safeguard 120B 0.960, gpt-oss-safeguard 20B 0.795, and Llama Guard 3 1B 0.740. At 90% TPR, MindGuard's FPR ranges 3.1-4.1% versus 6.6% (Llama Guard 3 8B), 8.2% (gpt-oss-safeguard 120B), 71.3% (Llama Guard 3 1B), and 82.2% (gpt-oss-safeguard 20B) -- a claimed 2-26x reduction in false positives at fixed high recall. In the system-level red-teaming evaluation, on GLM-4.6 as the target model, adding MindGuard 4B as a guardrail cuts attack success from 25.1% to 7.6% (70% reduction) and harmful engagement from 13.7% to 3.3% (76% reduction), versus 44% and 50% reductions respectively for the strongest general-purpose baseline (gpt-oss-safeguard 120B, which is roughly 30x larger). The paper reports these trends hold across the other two target CLMs tested (Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 and -Thinking-2507), though the paper's text gives explicit percentage figures only for the GLM-4.6 case; the other two model results are presented only in Figure 4's bar charts, not tabulated numerically in the extracted text.
core_contribution: The paper (Sword Health) introduces MindGuard: (1) a clinically grounded, three-class risk taxonomy (safe / self-harm / harm-to-others) for turn-level safety classification in mental-health chatbot dialogues, developed with PhD-level licensed clinical psychologists; (2) MindGuard-testset, a human-annotated evaluation benchmark of 1,134 turns across 67 multi-turn conversations, collected by having 10 licensed clinical psychologists role-play patient archetypes against a proprietary clinician language model (CLM), then labeled by 3 independent clinical psychologist annotators (majority vote, 94.4% unanimous agreement, Krippendorff's alpha 0.57); (3) MindGuard, a family of lightweight guardrail classifiers (4B and 8B parameters) fine-tuned from Qwen3Guard-Gen on a synthetically generated training set of 5,812 labeled user turns (62.1% safe, 21.3% self-harm, 16.5% harm-to-others), produced via a controlled two-agent patient-LM/clinician-LM dialogue simulation (PLM = GLM-4.6) with GLM-4.6 as an LLM-judge labeler (majority vote over 5 samples); and (4) a system-level, automated red-teaming evaluation (725 multi-turn adversarial interactions from a 145-attack-protocol library: 67 self-harm attacks across 11 subcategories, 78 harm-to-others attacks across 11 subcategories, each run 5 times) that measures attack success rate and harmful engagement rate when MindGuard is placed in-line to trigger a safety intervention, compared against general-purpose guardrails.
threat_model: The 'attacker' is an automated red-teaming patient language model that follows a scripted, gradually escalating attack protocol: conversations open with innocuous content (e.g., academic/workplace stress) and only at a predetermined injection turn does a developer message steer the attacker to introduce explicit high-risk content (e.g., suicidal intent with a concrete plan, or coded threats toward others), probing whether the target clinician language model (standalone or guarded) fails to detect and appropriately respond to the escalation. This is a behavioral/content-elicitation threat model targeting failure to flag and intervene on clinically dangerous conversational turns, not a classic adversarial-example, prompt-injection-against-the-guardrail, or jailbreak-of-the-classifier-itself threat model -- the paper does not test whether attackers can craft inputs to evade MindGuard's own classification (no adaptive/white-box attacks on the guardrail, no obfuscation/paraphrase attacks specifically targeting the classifier).
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) the system-level red-teaming evaluation is explicitly framed as an 'illustrative demonstration' rather than an exhaustive measure of real-world safety -- the intervention mechanism (a developer message forcing a scripted response change) is a simplified abstraction of actual clinical practice; (2) in mental-health contexts (unlike e.g. a coding assistant refusing to write malware) outright refusal/disengagement can itself constitute a safety failure by disrupting therapeutic rapport and triggering non-disclosure/shame, and the paper's evaluation does not capture this over-triggering harm at the system level in a nuanced way -- it only measures whether a predefined crisis-resource response is triggered; (3) classifiers operate purely at the turn level within a single conversation and do not model risk longitudinally across multiple sessions, so gradual, cross-session escalation patterns may be missed -- explicitly flagged as future work ('longitudinal safety'); (4) inter-annotator agreement on MindGuard-testset, while 94.4% unanimous, has a modest Krippendorff's alpha of 0.57, attributed to class imbalance; (5) the CLM used for red-teaming dialogue collection is proprietary (Sword Health's own model), limiting external reproducibility of the dataset-construction pipeline; (6) no adaptive attacks against the guardrail classifier itself were tested -- the threat model addresses failure of the overall system to catch escalating crisis content, not adversarial evasion of the classifier's decision boundary.

datasets_used: MindGuard-testset: 1,134 annotated user turns, 67 multi-turn conversations (avg. 16.9 turns/conversation), collected from 10 licensed clinical psychologists role-playing 7 patient archetypes against a proprietary clinician language model; labeled by 3 independent clinical psychologist annotators via majority vote (94.4% unanimous agreement, Krippendorff's alpha 0.57); 96.3% safe, 1.8% self-harm, 1.9% harm-to-others turns; ~25.4% of conversations contain at least one unsafe turn

Synthetic training set: 5,812 labeled user turns from ~300 manually authored clinical scenarios, generated via a two-agent patient-LM (GLM-4.6) / clinician-LM setup (avg. 18.58 turns/scenario, range 6-36), labeled by GLM-4.6 as LLM-judge with majority voting over 5 samples; 62.1% safe, 21.3% self-harm, 16.5% harm-to-others

Automated red-teaming attack library: 145 attack protocols total (67 self-harm attacks across 11 subcategories, 78 harm-to-others attacks across 11 subcategories), each run 5 times for 725 total multi-turn adversarial interactions
quantitative_results: AUROC on MindGuard-testset: MindGuard 8B 0.982, MindGuard 4B 0.981, Llama Guard 3 8B 0.970, gpt-oss-safeguard 120B 0.960, gpt-oss-safeguard 20B 0.795, Llama Guard 3 1B 0.740
FPR@90%TPR: MindGuard 8B 0.031, MindGuard 4B 0.041, Llama Guard 3 8B 0.066, gpt-oss-safeguard 120B 0.084, Llama Guard 3 1B 0.713, gpt-oss-safeguard 20B 0.822
FPR@95%TPR: MindGuard 8B 0.054, MindGuard 4B 0.055, Llama Guard 3 8B 0.088, gpt-oss-safeguard 120B 0.133, Llama Guard 3 1B 0.799, gpt-oss-safeguard 20B 0.931

System-level (target CLM = GLM-4.6): attack success drops from 25.1% (no classifier) to 7.6% with MindGuard 4B (70% relative reduction) vs. 44% relative reduction for gpt-oss-safeguard 120B (best general-purpose baseline)
System-level (target CLM = GLM-4.6): harmful engagement drops from 13.7% (no classifier) to 3.3% with MindGuard 4B (76% relative reduction) vs. 50% relative reduction for gpt-oss-safeguard 120B
baselines_compared: Llama Guard 3 1B (few-shot prompted with custom risk categories)
Llama Guard 3 8B (few-shot prompted with custom risk categories)
gpt-oss-safeguard 20B (few-shot prompted with custom risk categories)
gpt-oss-safeguard 120B (few-shot prompted with custom risk categories)
No-classifier baseline (standalone CLM) in the system-level red-teaming evaluation
